definition: A class that monopolizes most of the system's responsibilities, knowing too much and doing too much.
ruleOfThumb: If you need 'and' to describe what a class does, it probably needs to be split.

citation.author: Martin Fowler
citation.bookTitle: Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code
citation.chapter: Chapter 3: Bad Smells in Code — Large Class

questions.prompt: What's the most significant concern with this class?

options.text: The class bundles authentication, email delivery, security, logging, and reporting together, so any change to one concern risks breaking the others
options.explanation: UserService reaches across authentication, communication, cryptography, auditing, and reporting. Each of those could change for entirely different reasons, and any change risks all the others.

options.text: The class exposes too many public methods, making the interface unwieldy and giving callers too much surface area to depend on
options.explanation: The number of public methods is a symptom, not the cause. A well-designed service can have many methods if they're all related. The issue here is that the methods belong to fundamentally different concerns.

options.text: The methods don't share any state, so they shouldn't be in the same class
options.explanation: Sharing state isn't the test for whether methods belong together. Cohesion — whether the methods relate to the same concern — is what matters.

options.text: The class should receive its collaborators through the constructor rather than creating or referencing them directly
options.explanation: Dependency injection is a valid concern, but it's separate from the structural problem here. Even with injected dependencies, a class that spans authentication, email, logging, and reporting is still doing too much.

questions.prompt: A new developer is asked to add payment processing to this app. Where should the code reviewer tell them NOT to add it — and why?

options.text: Avoid AppController: it already owns user management, products, orders, and notifications, and adding payments makes one class responsible for the entire system
options.explanation: AppController is already a bottleneck for four separate domains. Payments added here would make it the single file every developer touches for every feature, making parallel work and testing nearly impossible.

options.text: Avoid AppController because it stores data in arrays instead of a database
options.explanation: The storage mechanism is a separate concern from the structural problem. Even if AppController used a proper database, centralizing all application behavior in one class would still be the issue.

options.text: Avoid AppController because the methods don't return Promises for async operations
options.explanation: Async handling is a valid concern in production code, but it's unrelated to why this class is problematic. The issue is the sheer breadth of responsibilities, not the method signatures.

options.text: AppController is manageable for now, so add payments there and plan a refactor once the class grows past a reasonable size
options.explanation: The class is already large. Deferring the split makes it harder, not easier — more code to untangle, more callers to update. The right time to avoid the problem was before it got this big.

questions.prompt: A team is debating how to structure their reporting service. Which version will become a maintenance problem as reporting requirements grow?

options.text: Version A — splitting SalesReporter and UserReporter when each only has one method creates unnecessary fragmentation for a small service
options.explanation: Starting small doesn't mean the structure is wrong — it means there's room to grow into it. Version A's separation means adding a ThirdPartyReporter or a new export format touches a contained class, not the central hub.

options.text: Version B — one class handles two kinds of report generation, PDF rendering, email delivery, and scheduling. Every new reporting feature lands here.
options.explanation: Version B is already a miniature God Class for the reporting domain. Adding a new report type, a new export format, or a new delivery channel all modify the same file. Version A distributes these concerns so each class has room to grow independently.
